verbatim_quote: So there are, you know, there are two divisions, you know, that I directly manage. Right. So basically, you know, just, you know, managing the affairs of those two divisions. Right. Okay. That's. That takes up, you know, like, a chunk of my time. This is customer success, you know, and so customer success has four teams. So basically, you know, like, this takes up a chunk of my time.
summary_of_observation: The participant directly manages seven teams across two divisions, consuming a significant portion of their time.

verbatim_quote: But apart from that, you know, like, from a strategic standpoint, you know, there are two main things, you know, that I keep my eye on. Keeping the customers. Right. And getting new customers. So basically, Sean Upsell. Right, okay. Growth of our current customers and getting new customers.

verbatim_quote: Client and prospect interaction, you know, is like a lot of what I do.
summary_of_observation: A significant portion of the participant's role involves interacting with clients and prospects.

verbatim_quote: attrition does affect our company as a whole in many different ways.

verbatim_quote: So one of the things in that I want to see, right, immediately is how much of my attrition is regrettable versus unregrettable. And this is a very important metric.
summary_of_observation: The participant immediately wants to know the breakdown of attrition into 'regrettable' versus 'unregrettable' as it's a very important metric for them.

verbatim_quote: So the term that we use in strut is regrettable, regrettable and unregrettable. It sounds better.
summary_of_observation: The participant prefers the terms 'regrettable' and 'unregrettable' for attrition analysis, finding them to sound better than desirable/undesirable.

verbatim_quote: It's really performance based. Performance and attitude based. That's it. The manager or the immediate supervisor knows already.
summary_of_observation: The determination of regrettable vs. unregrettable attrition is primarily based on an employee's performance and attitude, often known by their direct manager.

verbatim_quote: basically if you're looking at someone's performance holistically, there are a number of signals that you will look at, right? There are. There are a number of data points. You might be looking at their attendance, right? Like, you know, like, how. How tardy is this person? Okay, how many leaves do they take? What is the distribution of leaves like, etc, etc. Right? You will look at, you know, their output, right? And, you know, then you will look at, you know, like, generally speaking, you know, when you engage with them, how. How are they? Like, are they dull? But collectively, you know, the manager, whether you Know or not it's officially documented somewhere. Right. A good manager knows already that, okay, the, this, these are the people in my team, you know, who are basically meeting the metrics and these are the people who don't like.
summary_of_observation: Managers assess employee performance holistically using quantitative data points like attendance and output, and qualitative observations like energy and enthusiasm.

verbatim_quote: No, no, no, I said two divisions.

verbatim_quote: given the criticality of this role, even one designation is a problem for me. These clients give us the money to run as a company, to survive as a company. Right. So any one of them leave, it's going to be a big problem.
summary_of_observation: The departure of even one Enterprise CSM is a significant problem due to the criticality of their role in managing the biggest and most revenue-generating clients.

verbatim_quote: A customer success manager's role is relationship driven if you, if you know, because that enterprise customer success manager really spends time, you know, building relationship with different people, you know, with the, on the client side, when you remove that person, somebody new has to step in and rebuild those relationships. It doesn't happen overnight.
summary_of_observation: Customer Success Manager roles are relationship-driven, and losing such an individual requires a lengthy process for a new person to rebuild client relationships, which doesn't happen quickly.

verbatim_quote: Any good engineer, you know, would understand the code base, understand the architecture, understand, you know, how work is actually done in sprout. Right. If you take that engineer out. Right. Anybody new who comes in will have to go through that learning process over.

verbatim_quote: if it takes one year, that means anytime you lose a person, you know, it sets you back by two years.
summary_of_observation: The participant estimates that losing a key person could set the company back by two years due to the time required for a new hire to become effective and the lost productivity during that period.

verbatim_quote: Because if somebody resigned now, the new person, assuming that new person joins now, that new person is only effective a year from now. Right. And in that year, you know, while you're training this person, you also lost all the work that the other person would have done.
summary_of_observation: The participant's mental model for the impact of attrition includes a 'two-year' setback, accounting for both the ramp-up time of a new hire and the lost productivity of the departed employee.

verbatim_quote: Now what's missing here, you know, is that 12.5 and 2.3% are percentages. You should put a number in parentheses there. Okay. The 12.5% in the current quarter. Right. So, you know, like a quantity, like, you know, like absolute values that, you know, It's. Oh, it's 16 people this quarter versus, you know, 15 last quarter.
summary_of_observation: The participant wants to see absolute headcount numbers alongside percentages in the AI overview for clarity and immediate understanding of the scale of attrition.

verbatim_quote: I don't know. It could be. But, you know, like, I would assume that given the number of separations we are talking about, I would assume, you know, that it's like last 12 months or something.
summary_of_observation: Given the number of separations, the participant assumes 'last period' refers to the last 12 months, highlighting an ambiguity in the current labeling.

summary_of_observation: The participant expresses concern that the number of separations would be 'crazy high' if 'last period' referred to the last quarter, indicating a desire for clarity on the metric's timeframe.

verbatim_quote: I would want to see that, you know, I mean, like, regrettable versus unregulable.
summary_of_observation: The participant reiterates the strong need to see attrition broken down into 'regrettable' versus 'unregrettable' categories.

verbatim_quote: one problem, you know, when it comes to showing attrition by percentages. Right. Okay. Or only percentages, is that there are. There is a department in Sprout that has only four people. If one of them resigns, you will say that the retrition is 33.3%.
summary_of_observation: The participant identifies a problem with showing attrition solely by percentages, as it can be misleading for small departments (e.g., one resignation in a four-person team shows 33.3% attrition).

verbatim_quote: So that's why, you know, it has to be. It has to be balanced, you know, by count as well. Yeah. Like, relative to the size of the department. Right. So, you know, you can also see that, okay, 10 people out of 40 people left or two people out of 40 people left.

summary_of_observation: The participant emphasizes the high cost associated with hiring and onboarding new employees, reinforcing the importance of monitoring probationary attrition.
